instruction: Make this ball bounce when it hits the floor
assumptions: "this ball" refers to the GameObject named "Sphere_01".
"the floor" refers to any GameObject that "Sphere_01" collides with, provided the collision normal points predominantly upwards (Y-component greater than 'floorNormalThreshold').

The desired "bounce" behavior is simulated by applying an impulse to "Sphere_01"'s Rigidbody upon collision with the "floor". The impulse is calculated based on the ball's incoming velocity along the collision normal and a 'bounciness' coefficient, effectively reversing and scaling the component of velocity perpendicular to the surface.
The GameObject "Sphere_01" has a Rigidbody component attached to it, as indicated in the scene description.
The default values for 'bounciness' (0.8f), 'floorNormalThreshold' (0.7f), and 'minImpactVelocityForBounce' (0.1f) are suitable starting points and can be adjusted via the Inspector.

instruction: Make this object fall slowly, like it's underwater
assumptions: The user wants the 'falling slowly' effect applied to 'Cube_01' since it's a generic grabbable physics object and no specific object was mentioned.
The 'underwater' effect is achieved by increasing the Rigidbody's drag and angular drag to simulate fluid resistance.
A constant upward force is added to simulate buoyancy, further contributing to the 'falling slowly' effect by partially counteracting gravity.

The chosen default values for 'underwaterDrag' (5f), 'underwaterAngularDrag' (5f), and 'buoyancyForceMagnitude' (1f) provide a reasonable starting point for an 'underwater' feel in Unity's default physics environment.

instruction: Make this cube turn red when the player grabs it
code: using UnityEngine;using UnityEngine.XR.Interaction.Toolkit;public class GrabColorChanger : MonoBehaviour{private MeshRenderer meshRenderer;private Color originalColor;private XRGrabInteractable grabInteractable;void Awake(){meshRenderer = GetComponent<MeshRenderer>();if (meshRenderer != null){originalColor = meshRenderer.material.color;}grabInteractable = GetComponent<XRGrabInteractable>();}void OnEnable(){if (grabInteractable != null){grabInteractable.selectEntered.AddListener(OnGrabbed);grabInteractable.selectExited.AddListener(OnReleased);}}void OnDisable(){if (grabInteractable != null){grabInteractable.selectEntered.RemoveListener(OnGrabbed);grabInteractable.selectExited.RemoveListener(OnReleased);}}private void OnGrabbed(SelectEnterEventArgs args){if (meshRenderer != null){meshRenderer.material.color = Color.red;}}private void OnReleased(SelectExitEventArgs args){if (meshRenderer != null){meshRenderer.material.color = originalColor;}}}
assumptions: The 'Cube_01' GameObject will have an 'XRGrabInteractable' component attached to it, which is part of the Unity XR Interaction Toolkit, to detect grabbing and releasing events.
The 'GrabColorChanger' script will be attached to the 'Cube_01' GameObject.
The 'Cube_01' GameObject's color should revert to its original color when the player releases it, providing a complete interaction cycle.

instruction: Make this lever rotate 90 degrees when pulled
code: using UnityEngine;public class LeverRotationController : MonoBehaviour{ public float rotationRange = 90f; void Start() { HingeJoint hingeJoint = GetComponent<HingeJoint>(); if (hingeJoint != null) { JointLimits limits = hingeJoint.limits; limits.min = 0f; limits.max = rotationRange; hingeJoint.limits = limits; hingeJoint.useLimits = true; } else { Debug.LogError("HingeJoint not found on " + gameObject.name); } }}
assumptions: The 'Lever_01' is intended to be the target object for this script.
The 'HingeJoint' component on 'Lever_01' is already configured with the correct anchor and axis for rotation, and only its limits need to be set.
The 'pulled' action implies that the rotation of the lever should be constrained within a 90-degree range by setting the 'HingeJoint' limits.
The 90-degree rotation range is set from a minimum of 0 degrees to a maximum of 90 degrees relative to the HingeJoint's initial orientation.

instruction: Make this object glow when the player looks at it
code: using UnityEngine;public class GazeGlow : MonoBehaviour{ private Material originalMaterial; private Color originalEmissionColor; private bool isGlowing; public Color glowColor = Color.yellow; public float glowIntensity = 1f; public float raycastDistance = 100f; void Start(){ MeshRenderer meshRenderer = GetComponent<MeshRenderer>(); if (meshRenderer != null){ originalMaterial = meshRenderer.material; originalEmissionColor = originalMaterial.HasProperty("_EmissionColor") ? originalMaterial.GetColor("_EmissionColor") : Color.black; } else{ Debug.LogError("GazeGlow requires a MeshRenderer component.", this); enabled = false; } } void Update(){ RaycastHit hit; if (Physics.Raycast(Camera.main.transform.position, Camera.main.transform.forward, out hit, raycastDistance)){ if (hit.collider.gameObject == gameObject){ if (!isGlowing){ SetGlow(true); } } else{ if (isGlowing){ SetGlow(false); } } } else{ if (isGlowing){ SetGlow(false); } } } private void SetGlow(bool enable){ MeshRenderer meshRenderer = GetComponent<MeshRenderer>(); if (meshRenderer == null){ return; } Material currentMaterial = meshRenderer.material; if (enable){ currentMaterial.EnableKeyword("_EMISSION"); currentMaterial.SetColor("_EmissionColor", glowColor * glowIntensity); } else{ currentMaterial.SetColor("_EmissionColor", originalEmissionColor); if (originalEmissionColor == Color.black){ currentMaterial.DisableKeyword("_EMISSION"); } } isGlowing = enable; } private void OnDestroy(){ if (originalMaterial != null){ originalMaterial.SetColor("_EmissionColor", originalEmissionColor); if (originalEmissionColor == Color.black){ originalMaterial.DisableKeyword("_EMISSION"); } } }}
assumptions: "this object" refers to the GameObject `Cube_01`.
"player" refers to the `Player` GameObject which contains the main `Camera`.
"looks at it" implies a raycast from the player's camera forward vector.
"glow" means changing the `_EmissionColor` property of the object's material and enabling the `_EMISSION` keyword if needed.
The target object `Cube_01` has a `MeshRenderer` component to access its material.
The material on `Cube_01` supports `_EmissionColor` and the `_EMISSION` keyword (standard for Unity's default materials or URP/HDRP lit shaders).
The default glow color is yellow with an intensity of 1.
The raycast distance to detect the gaze is 100 units.
The script is attached directly to the `Cube_01` GameObject.

instruction: Make this panel fade out after 3 seconds
code: using UnityEngine;using UnityEngine.UI;using System.Collections;public class PanelFadeOut : MonoBehaviour{    public float fadeDelay = 3f;    public float fadeDuration = 1f;    private RawImage rawImage;    void Start()    {        rawImage = GetComponent<RawImage>();        if (rawImage == null)        {            Debug.LogError("RawImage component not found on " + gameObject.name);            return;        }        StartCoroutine(FadeOutAfterDelay());    }    IEnumerator FadeOutAfterDelay()    {        yield return new WaitForSeconds(fadeDelay);        Color currentColor = rawImage.color;        float startAlpha = currentColor.a;        float timer = 0f;        while (timer < fadeDuration)        {            timer += Time.deltaTime;            float newAlpha = Mathf.Lerp(startAlpha, 0f, timer / fadeDuration);            rawImage.color = new Color(currentColor.r, currentColor.g, currentColor.b, newAlpha);            yield return null;        }        rawImage.color = new Color(currentColor.r, currentColor.g, currentColor.b, 0f);        gameObject.SetActive(false);    }}
assumptions: The script should be attached to 'Panel_01'.
The fade out duration is assumed to be 1 second, as no duration was specified.
The panel should become inactive after fading out completely.

instruction: Make this door open when the player walks within 1 meter
assumptions: The 'Door_01' opens by translating upwards along its local Y-axis.
The 'Player' GameObject is the entity whose transform position is used for distance calculation.
If the 'player' field is not manually assigned in the Inspector, the script will attempt to find a GameObject named 'Player' in the scene.
The door will move 3 units upwards from its initial position when fully open.
The door will open and close at a speed of 1 unit per second.

instruction: Make this object follow the player's hand at a distance
code: using UnityEngine;using UnityEngine.XR;using System.Collections.Generic;public class HandFollower : MonoBehaviour{public float followDistance = 0.5f;private InputDevice rightHandDevice;void Start(){InitializeRightHand();}{void Update(){if (!rightHandDevice.isValid){InitializeRightHand();}if (rightHandDevice.isValid){Vector3 handPosition;Quaternion handRotation;if (rightHandDevice.TryGetFeatureValue(CommonUsages.devicePosition, out handPosition) && rightHandDevice.TryGetFeatureValue(CommonUsages.deviceRotation, out handRotation)){transform.position = handPosition + handRotation * Vector3.forward * followDistance;transform.rotation = handRotation;}}}}{void InitializeRightHand(){List<InputDevice> devices = new List<InputDevice>();InputDevices.GetDevicesWithCharacteristics(InputDeviceCharacteristics.Right | InputDeviceCharacteristics.Controller, devices);if (devices.Count > 0){rightHandDevice = devices[0];Debug.Log("Right hand device found: " + rightHandDevice.name);}}}}
assumptions: The script `HandFollower` will be attached to 'Cube_01'.
"Player's hand" refers to the player's right-hand XR controller.
The object should follow the hand's position and rotation, maintaining `followDistance` in front of the controller's forward direction.
The default `followDistance` is 0.5 units.
The XR controller input is available via `UnityEngine.XR.InputDevices` and `CommonUsages`.
The XR device is properly initialized and tracking its position and rotation.

instruction: When this button is pressed, turn on that light
assumptions: The 'Button_01' GameObject will have this 'ButtonLightController' script attached.
The 'Button_01' GameObject's BoxCollider will be set to 'Is Trigger' in the Inspector to detect a 'press' event via OnTriggerEnter.
The 'Light_01' GameObject will be dragged into the 'Target Light' field of the 'ButtonLightController' script in the Inspector.
A 'press' of the button is registered when any collider enters the trigger collider of 'Button_01'.
Turning on the light means setting the `enabled` property of its Light component to `true`.

instruction: Make this object copy the rotation of that other object
assumptions: The script will be attached to "Cube_01".
"Cube_01" is "this object" mentioned in the instruction.
"Sphere_01" is "that other object" mentioned in the instruction.
The rotation copying should happen continuously, so it is implemented in the `Update` method.
The user will manually assign the `Sphere_01`'s Transform to the `targetTransform` field in the Unity Editor after the script is added to `Cube_01`.

instruction: Make the thing move when I grab it
assumptions: The "thing" refers to "Cube_01" because it is the first object tagged "grabbable" in the scene.
"Grab" implies using Unity's XR Interaction Toolkit's `Select` interaction event.
"Move when I grab it" means the object should directly follow the grabbing controller's position and rotation while being held.
The target object "Cube_01" requires a `Rigidbody` and an `XRGrabInteractable` component for this script to function and detect grab events, which are added automatically by `RequireComponent`.

instruction: Make it bigger when I look at it
assumptions: The instruction 'it' refers to the object named 'Cube_01'.
The player's gaze is determined by a raycast from the `Camera.main` object.
The camera component on the 'Player' GameObject is tagged 'MainCamera', allowing it to be accessed via `Camera.main`.
Making the object 'bigger' implies a uniform scale increase by a factor of 1.2 along all axes.
The object should return to its original size when the player's gaze is no longer on it.
